---
title: "AI as Research Infrastructure"
subtitle: "A Case Study in Dissertation-Scale Computational Science Using GEN/PAI/Claude Code"
author:
  - name: Michael Bean
    affiliation: Independent Researcher
    corresponding: true
date: "2026-03-25"
abstract: |
  This paper documents the research process behind a doctoral dissertation
  comprising five papers and one methodological supplement in computational
  artificial life, produced over twelve days using GEN, a personal AI
  assistant built on PAI (Personal AI Infrastructure) running on Claude Code
  with Claude Opus 4.6. The process involved autonomous experiment design
  and distributed cluster execution (approximately 15,000 simulation jobs
  producing 4.6 million session-level observations), simulated dissertation committee
  meetings with persistent AI personas, AI-conducted peer review,
  comprehensive parallel literature search across five API backends, a
  multi-phase academic integrity audit, and a mock doctoral defence with
  external examiners. We describe the technical architecture, the roles
  played by AI agents at each stage, failure modes encountered, and
  findings that converged with prior literature the system had not consulted. The
  case raises questions about the nature of discovery when the researcher,
  committee, reviewers, and auditors operate as AI systems under the
  direction of a single human principal.
keywords:
  - AI-assisted research
  - personal AI infrastructure
  - computational artificial life
  - research methodology
  - academic process
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    fig-width: 7
    fig-height: 5
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: a4
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - margin=2.5cm
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
bibliography: meta-references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa.csl
---

# Introduction

None of this was planned.

On March 13, 2026, Michael Bean opened a conversation with GEN --- his
personal AI assistant --- about Deleuze's concept of *differenciation*
and whether goalless dynamics could produce qualitatively different kinds
from identical starting conditions. There was no research agenda, no
proposal, no intention to write a paper. It was a late-night question
driven by curiosity: could a Hebbian network, left entirely to its own
devices, produce something that Deleuze would recognise as genuine
difference?

The conversation led to code. The code produced results that were
interesting enough to write up. Writing up raised questions that required
more experiments. The experiments revealed a confound that changed the
interpretation of everything that came before. At each step, the decision
to continue was made in the moment, not according to a plan. The
"dissertation" framing emerged organically when the accumulated work
reached the scale where it needed a structure --- and the simulated
committee, peer review, and defence emerged as tools for pressure-testing
that structure, not as ends in themselves.

Michael's background informed the direction at every turn. His interest
in Deleuze and Simondon shaped the philosophical framework. His
frustration with metrics that measure the wrong thing led to the CCD
invalidation and the adoption of response rank. His instinct that "the
pruning shouldn't matter" led to the experiment that showed it mattered
enormously. The AI system executed and elaborated, but the questions ---
and the dissatisfaction with easy answers --- came from the human.

Twelve days later, the conversation had produced approximately 15,000
simulation jobs on a 20$\times$20-node substrate, 4.6 million
session-level observations, five completed papers, a dissertation
assembly, and a simulated defence. This document exists because the
process itself turned out to be as interesting as the findings.

**What this document is not.** It is not a formal methods paper. It is
not a proposal for how AI-assisted research should be conducted. It is a
record of how one human and one AI system followed a question where it
led, using the tools available, and what they found along the way ---
both about Hebbian self-organisation and about the process of
AI-assisted inquiry.

**What was real and what was simulated.** The computational experiments
executed on a physical Ray cluster and produced real data. The scientific
findings are genuine results of those computations. The academic process
surrounding them --- committee meetings, peer review, editorial
decisions, and the doctoral defence --- was entirely simulated using AI
agents within a single conversation session. No papers were submitted to
any journal. No committee held institutional authority. No defence had
academic standing. The simulated processes served as quality-control
mechanisms and stress tests, not as claims of academic legitimacy.

# The Technical Stack {#sec-stack}

## GEN and PAI

GEN is the human principal's personal AI assistant, built on the Personal
AI Infrastructure (PAI) framework created by Daniel Miessler
[@miessler2024pai]. PAI extends Claude Code [@anthropic2025claudecode] with
capabilities that proved essential for sustained research:

**Agent orchestration.** PAI enables spawning specialised sub-agents ---
Architect, Engineer, Explore, and multiple researcher types (Codex, Gemini,
Grok, Perplexity, Claude) --- for parallel task execution. Each agent
operates with its own context but inherits the underlying model. This
architecture enabled the parallel committee meetings, fourteen-agent
literature reviews, and multi-phase audits documented below.

**Persistent memory.** A file-based memory system preserves project
context, learning signals, and work items across conversation sessions.
Two research documents that survived from the programme's first day ---
"Capacities, Intent, and Affordances" (a synthesis of Gibson and DeLanda)
and "Qualitative Differentiation from Homogeneous Substrates" (a reading
of Deleuze's *Difference and Repetition*) --- demonstrate that
philosophical research preceded and shaped the experimental design.

**Skill routing.** Modular capabilities invoked by slash commands provide
structured workflows. The Research skill, for example, routes to
appropriate agent configurations (quick, standard, extensive, or deep
investigation) based on the scope of the query.

**The Algorithm.** A structured multi-phase reasoning protocol for complex
tasks that enforces systematic observation, planning, and execution rather
than immediate response.

## Claude Code and Claude Opus 4.6

Claude Code [@anthropic2025claudecode] provides the runtime: direct
filesystem access, bash execution, git operations, and multi-tool
orchestration within a persistent working directory. The underlying model,
Claude Opus 4.6, offers a one-million-token context window --- a
constraint that proved load-bearing. This session accumulated context
across experiment monitoring, six committee meetings, five peer review
rounds, a fourteen-agent literature synthesis, and paper revision, all
within a single continuous conversation.

## The Polyglot Pipeline

The choice of Quarto [@allaire2024quarto] as the publishing system was
driven by a practical requirement: the substrate simulations are Python
(numpy, scipy, executed on the Ray cluster), while the statistical analysis
and publication figures are R (ggplot2, arrow, patchwork). Quarto renders
both languages in a single `.qmd` document.

The data interchange format, Apache Parquet, bridges the two ecosystems.
Python cluster jobs write per-seed Parquet files; R reads them directly via
the `arrow` package. Parquet's columnar storage and typed schema prevent
the format drift common with CSV interchange. The complete experimental
record (approximately 4.6 million session-level observations) compresses to 40 megabytes.

## Distributed Computation

Ray [@moritz2018ray] provided the cluster computing layer. A three-node
cluster (68 CPUs: 32 on a workstation, 24 on a second node, 12 on the head
node) executed all simulation jobs. GEN submitted experiments to the
cluster directly from the conversation via Ray's HTTP job submission API,
monitored progress through log polling, and retrieved results by reading
Parquet files from a shared NFS mount. When the laptop node disconnected
during overnight runs, Ray automatically retried failed tasks on the
remaining nodes.

# The Simulated Committee {#sec-committee}

Three AI committee members were instantiated from written character
descriptions and accumulated meeting transcripts. Each meeting was a fresh
agent invocation conditioned on this context, not a persistent process
with continuous memory. Within these constraints, the personas produced
differentiated intellectual positions:

**Dr. Elena Vasquez** (Chair, Complex Systems, Santa Fe Institute) ---
pragmatic, scope-focused, protective of timeline. Made decisive calls on
submission timing. Warning against scope creep proved prescient when the
literature review expanded the revision plan.

**Dr. James Okafor** (Computational Neuroscience, UCL) --- statistically
demanding. Raised the two concerns (pruning confound and CCD invalidity)
that reshaped the programme. His insistence on nested ANOVA, Wilcoxon
robustness checks, and deconfounded probes improved the statistical
foundation.

**Dr. Marie-Claire Dupont** (Philosophy of Technology, EGS) ---
philosophically precise. Her correction of "structural germ" to "limit
condition" was later validated by an external examiner. Her framing of the
"productive fracture" became the programme's central philosophical
contribution.

## Committee Dynamics

The committee's contribution was structural rather than informational:
when members disagreed, the disagreement produced better decisions than
any individual perspective. Vasquez's pragmatism moderated Okafor's
experimental ambition; Dupont's precision corrected Vasquez's tendency to
dismiss philosophical contributions as inessential.

Positions shifted across meetings because each subsequent invocation
included the transcripts of prior meetings as context. Okafor's partial
concession on "phantom individuation," Vasquez's shift from leading with
the confound to leading with the steepness result, and Dupont's
acceptance that the autopoiesis claim required functional validation were
conditioned on prior exchanges, not on persistent memory or genuine
intellectual development. Whether this constitutes "evolution" of
positions or sophisticated context-matching is an open question.

# Simulated Peer Review {#sec-review}

Each paper submission involved three AI reviewers with distinct expertise,
an editorial decision, a response letter, and revision. The process
produced genuine quality improvement.

## Issues Identified

Reviewers identified legitimate problems:

- CCD invalidation was underspecified (Reviewer 1, Paper 4S)
- The edge cap mechanism potentially reintroduced the pruning confound
  (Reviewer 2, Paper 4S --- described as "the most dangerous item")
- "Structural germ" was an incorrect Simondonian category (Reviewer 3,
  Paper 4S)
- Critical period and perturbation resilience lacked functional validation
  (Reviewers 1 and 2, Paper 4)

## Revision Experiments

Addressing reviewer concerns required 900 additional simulation jobs and
three new figures. This mirrors real peer review, where revision
experiments often exceed the original submission in scope.

## Reviewer Errors

The system was not infallible. One proofreading agent incorrectly flagged
verified data as wrong. A reviewer for Paper 4 read the companion Paper
4S and confused cross-paper references. Some questions were answerable
from the manuscript itself. These failures are documented because they
illustrate the limitations of simulated review.

# The Literature Review {#sec-literature}

A parallel deployment of fourteen research agents across five API backends
(Claude, Gemini, Grok, Perplexity, and domain-specific contextualizers)
found that approximately forty to fifty percent of major results had
substantial prior art.

| Finding | Prior Art |
|---------|-----------|
| Pruning destroys diversity | Hooker et al. [-@hooker2019compressed] |
| Critical periods emerge | Achille et al. [-@achille2019critical] |
| Response rank as metric | Stringer et al. [-@stringer2019high] |
| Self-sealing resembles autopoiesis | Maturana and Varela [-@maturana1980autopoiesis] |
| Fixed rules cannot produce novelty | Adams et al. [-@adams2017formal] |

: Convergent findings and their prior art. {#tbl-convergence}

The committee reframed this convergence: independent derivation of
established results from an unrelated starting point constitutes evidence
that the underlying phenomena are real, not artifacts of any single
methodology. The genuinely novel findings --- the disordinal interaction,
the non-monotonic steepness result, the critical threshold boundaries,
and the grid-size scaling law --- survived the prior art assessment.

# The Academic Audit {#sec-audit}

A coordinating agent executed a seven-phase audit, spawning specialised
sub-agents for each verification task:

1. Citation verification (web search for DOIs and claim accuracy)
2. Code-data consistency (parquet files against paper claims)
3. Statistical methods (reproduction of tests from raw values)
4. Internal consistency (term and number concordance across documents)
5. Philosophical terminology (correct deployment of Simondonian concepts)
6. Reproducibility (tracing claim-to-data chains through source code)
7. Prose quality (logical coherence and argumentative structure)

The audit caught errors that multiple rounds of proofreading had missed:
a Shapiro-Wilk p-value reported as 0.40 instead of 0.99, a dissertation
framing document with stale terminology and outdated numbers, and a
duplicated author name in a bibliography entry.

# The Mock Defence {#sec-defence}

Two external examiners were introduced as AI-generated personas (not the
actual scholars): "Prof. Randall Beer" (modelled on the Indiana University
researcher's published work in computational autopoiesis) and "Prof. Sara
Hooker" (modelled on the Cohere researcher's published work on compressed
network fairness). These names are used to indicate the expertise profile
of each persona, not to claim endorsement or participation by the real
individuals.

The most difficult exchange concerned whether self-sealing assemblages
constitute genuine individuals or merely spatial patterns in a continuous
field. The candidate's response --- that the assemblages possess measurable
properties and resist perturbation but are constituted by spatial context
rather than intrinsic properties --- identified the programme's deepest
philosophical vulnerability without fully resolving it.

Verdict: pass with minor corrections.

# What the System Did Well and Poorly {#sec-assessment}

## Strengths

Parallel execution proved valuable: fourteen literature agents running
simultaneously, cluster jobs executing overnight while writing proceeded,
multiple committee members debating in a single invocation. Persistent
context allowed committee positions to evolve across meetings. The system
identified errors during directed investigations: the pruning confound
through a human-requested experiment, the CCD invalidity through a
human-requested permutation test, and the Shapiro-Wilk miscalculation
through a human-requested audit.

## Weaknesses

Literature awareness was insufficient. A researcher familiar with the
neural network compression literature would have encountered Hooker et al.
before designing the pruning experiments. Metric selection was suboptimal:
four papers relied on centroid cosine distance before discovering it was
spatially confounded. Philosophical claims occasionally outran the
evidence: "structural germ" persisted through several drafts before
correction.

## The Role of the Human

One human directed every strategic decision: which questions to pursue,
when to run experiments or write, when to consult the committee, when to
conduct the literature review, and when to stop. The AI system executed,
analysed, wrote, reviewed, and audited. The division was consistent
throughout: the human chose the direction; the system travelled it.

# Implications {#sec-implications}

## For Research Practice

The committee disagreement format was the most productive architectural
choice. Multiple AI personas with distinct intellectual commitments,
debating a shared question, produced better outcomes than any individual
agent. This suggests that AI-assisted research benefits more from
structured *disagreement* than from optimised individual performance.

## For Academic Integrity

This programme raises questions without settled answers. Who is the author
when writing is AI-generated under human direction? How should simulated
peer review be disclosed? When an AI system independently derives a result
with prior art, is the appropriate response citation of the prior work,
acknowledgment of independent derivation, or both? This document exists
to make the process transparent so that others can evaluate it on its
merits.

## For Reproducibility

Every artifact in the accompanying repository --- code, data, papers,
committee transcripts, peer reviews, audit reports, and this document ---
is provided so that the process can be examined, critiqued, and, if
desired, replicated.

# References
